coredata.dc:title: Acid catalyzed microwave-assisted production of full-color light emissive carbon quantum dots for CCT-tunable WLEDs
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Bo-Seok
Oh, Geun-Hyeong
Song, Youngjun
Lee, Youn-Sik
Kim, Sejung
coredata.dc:description: Many studies have examined the use of multicolor light emissive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in toxic metal-free optoelectronic devices, but it is still limited to obtain desirable optical properties. Here, we present a straightforward synthetic route for producing multicolor light emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) using a microwave-assisted process, thus yielding individual color emissions of NCQDs in a batch. The emissive colors from NCQDs can be controlled by adjusting the molar ratios of citric acid and urea from 1 to 6 for blue and green-light emission, respectively. When o-phenylenediamine (oPDA) was chosen as a precursor, the resulting NCQDs can emit yellow light, while the red light emissive NCQDs can be obtained in the presence of a diluted HCl as a catalyst for accelerating and extending sp2 -carbon bonding. The doped nitrogen in NCQDs was found to be formed as a graphitic nitrogen in the core, leading to red-shifted photoluminescent emissions from 450 nm to 620 nm. To fabricate white-light emitting diodes (WLEDs), the mixing ratios of NCQDs with different light emissions in polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) were varied to control the correlated color temperature (CCT) from 3386 K to 7151 K and CIE coordinates of (0.30, 0.32), (0.32, 0.36), and (0.40, 0.37).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Multicolor light emission
Microwave reaction
Graphitic nitrogen
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
Preparation of full-color light conversion NCQDs films
Fabrication of NCQD-based light emitting diodes
Preparation of multicolor light emissive nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs)
Optical properties of multicolor light emission of NCQDs
Multicolor light emissive NCQDs-based light-emitting diodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government ( NRF-2021R1F1A1063451 and NRF-2021R1A43031992 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectra, PL emission spectra, and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra of (a) B-, (b) G-, (c) Y-, and (d) R-CQDs.
UPS spectra of (a) B-, (b) G-, (c) Y-, and (d) R-NCQDs. (left: the cutoff region, center: overall region, right: the onset region.), (e) energy bandgap diagram of NCQDs.
(a) TEM image of B-NCQDs (inset: HR-TEM image, lattice fringe), (b) size distribution of NCQDs.
High-resolution XPS analysis of (a) C 1s and (b) N 1s of B-, G-, Y-, and R-NCQDs.

CIE chromaticity coordinates of (a) B-, (b) G-, (c) Y-, and (d) R-NCQDs-based LEDs and corresponding photographs of NCQDs under UV lamp and CQDs-coated UV chips (at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm).

(a–c) CIE chromaticity coordinates of NCQDs-based WLEDs and corresponding photographs of CQDs under UV lamp and NCQDs-coated UV chips (at the excitation wavelength 365 nm) and (d–f) corresponding PL spectra of NCQDs based WLEDs.
Schematic diagram depicting the preparation of multicolor light emissive NCQDs.
Comparison of HOMO, LUMO, and bandgap energy levels of NCQDs.

head.ce:title: Acid catalyzed microwave-assisted production of full-color light emissive carbon quantum dots for CCT-tunable WLEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, School of Semiconductor and Chemical Engineering, Clean Energy Research Center, Jeonbuk National University, 567 Baekje-daero, Jeonju-si, Jeollabuk-do 54896, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Semiconductor and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, School of Semiconductor and Chemical Engineering, Clean Energy Research Center, Jeonbuk National University, 567 Baekje-daero, Jeonju-si, Jeollabuk-do 54896, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nano-bioengineering, Incheon National University, Academy-ro 119, Incheon 22012, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nano-bioengineering, Incheon National University, Academy-ro 119, Incheon 22012, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Many studies have examined the use of multicolor light emissive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in toxic metal-free optoelectronic devices, but it is still limited to obtain desirable optical properties. Here, we present a straightforward synthetic route for producing multicolor light emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) using a microwave-assisted process, thus yielding individual color emissions of NCQDs in a batch. The emissive colors from NCQDs can be controlled by adjusting the molar ratios of citric acid and urea from 1 to 6 for blue and green-light emission, respectively. When o-phenylenediamine (oPDA) was chosen as a precursor, the resulting NCQDs can emit yellow light, while the red light emissive NCQDs can be obtained in the presence of a diluted HCl as a catalyst for accelerating and extending sp2 -carbon bonding. The doped nitrogen in NCQDs was found to be formed as a graphitic nitrogen in the core, leading to red-shifted photoluminescent emissions from 450 nm to 620 nm. To fabricate white-light emitting diodes (WLEDs), the mixing ratios of NCQDs with different light emissions in polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) were varied to control the correlated color temperature (CCT) from 3386 K to 7151 K and CIE coordinates of (0.30, 0.32), (0.32, 0.36), and (0.40, 0.37).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: White-light emissive diodes (WLEDs) have been extensively studied in the field of lighting and display technologies due to their low electricity consumption as well as their semi-permanent and eco-friendly characteristics [1–3]. WLEDs are typically manufactured by stacking phosphors on blue light-emissive InGaN chips, wherein the phosphors serve as a color conversion layer from blue light to white light emission [4,5]. WLEDs are used in various fields such as backlight, mobile displays, medical, vehicle forward lighting and indoors lighting, etc. As applying in these various fields, it is important to have proper color correlated temperature (CCT) in WLEDs. Warm WLEDs have a low CCT (<4000 K) and are used for eye comfort and fatigue-relieving indoor lighting. Cool WLEDs have a high CCT (>7000 K) and are used for workplace or outdoor lighting [6]. There have been many studies focusing on the development of highly efficient materials for WLEDs, including rare earth-based materials, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), perovskite quantum dots and metal organic frameworks (MOFs). However, it is still challenging to use the developed materials in WLEDs due to their intrinsic toxicity, environmental destruction, stability, and challenging synthesis [7–12].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can overcome those limitations that are attributable to their favorable properties, such as their environmentally friendly, low toxicity, and tunable optical properties [13,14]. The tunable optical bandgaps of CQDs can be utilized as photocatalysts by forming hybrid structure with Mn3O4, NiO, WO3, and Bi2O3, resulting in highly efficient hydrogen production, and water purification [15–20]. The optimal way to tune the bandgaps of CQDs remains ambiguous; one is the quantum confinement effect (QCE) when the radius of CQDs is smaller than the Bohr radius, thus resulting in discontinuity of energy levels. As a result, smaller size CQDs have fewer π-conjugation domains, which results in a larger bandgap [21]. Another factor that is relevant in adjusting the bandgap of CQDs is the surface states, including heteroatom doping, or functional groups of CQDs [22–24]. Even though CQDs are likely to be used as a color conversion layer for WLEDs due to their tunable optical properties, there are still two major challenges limiting the use of CQDs in WLEDs [25]. First, it is essential to achieve an efficient synthetic process toward the mass production of each different light emission from CQDs. However, it has been reported that the synthesized CQDs should be separated into different light color emissive CQDs using a purification process such as column chromatography [26]. Moreover, the lack of synthesis strategies for producing red light emissive CQDs limits the ability to control the correlated color temperature and CIE coordination, which leads to a cool white light with low CRI [27,28]. Therefore, a strategy should be developed that can efficiently produce full-color light emissive CQDs with controllable sizes and π-conjugation domains by adjusting reaction conditions such as temperature, time, and solvents [29]. Second, photoluminescent quenching caused by the aggregation of solid-state CQDs leads to deterioration of the performance of WLEDs. To avoid luminescent quenching of CQDs, CQDs can be incorporated with polymer matrices or nanoparticles such as polyacrylonitrile (PAN), poly(acrylic acid) (PAA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), SiO2, TiO2, and starch [29–34].

Herein, we developed a synthetic process of CQDs toward their mass production through microwave-assisted synthesis without the need for a further purification process (e. g., column chromatography), thus yielding a single light emission in one batch. The microwave-assisted synthetic process can be used to simply produce nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) in a single light emission in one batch by generating heat from molecular rotation under electric fields within less than 15 min. Moreover, diluted HCl can be used as a catalyst to accelerate sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon bonding and extend their conjugation lengths to produce a longer emissive wavelength. Further, doped nitrogen can be formed into graphitic nitrogen structures in the core of CQDs to narrow the bandgap, thus resulting in red-shifted PL emission. Finally, multicolor light emissive NCQDs prepared in various ratios were composited with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), then coated with a 365 nm light emissive UV chip, resulting in the ability to adjust CCT among warm, natural, and cool WLEDs from 3386 K to 7151 K with the CIE coordinates of (0.30, 0.32), (0.32, 0.36), and (0.40, 0.37), respectively.

Citric acid (CA), urea, poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA, Mw ∼ 120 K), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. o-Phenylenediamine (oPDA) was purchased from ACROS ORGANICS. Ethyl alcohol, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 35.0–37.0 %) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, bead) were purchased from SAMCHUN CHEMICAL. Sylgard 184 silicone elastomer base and elastomer curing agent were purchased from DOW CHEMICAL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis, the multicolor light emissive CQDs were prepared through microwave irradiation (1000 W) at different conditions. For the synthesis of blue and green light emissive NCQDs, the molar ratios of citric acid and urea were adjusted. To obtain blue light emissive NCQDs (B-NCQDs), 2 mmol of citric acid and 2 mmol of urea were dispersed in 10 mL DI water and reacted in a microwave oven (MAS-II Plus, Sieno) at 180 °C for 10 min. After being allowed to cool down to room temperature, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 13,500 rpm for 10 min and filtered by a 0.22 μm PTFE membrane, followed by dialysis with a MWCO 3.5 kDa for 48 h to remove unreacted precursors. Finally, the dark-brown powdered B-NCQDs were obtained by freeze drying for 48 h. For the synthesis of green light emissive NCQDs (G-NCQDs), 0.52 mmol of citric acid and 3.3 mmol of urea were dispersed in 10 mL DI water, and graphitization proceeded in a microwave oven at 180 °C for 10 min. After the reaction, the purification and freeze-drying steps were followed along the same procedure to obtain B-NCQDs.

For the synthesis of yellow (Y-NCQDs) and red-light emissive NCQDs (R-NCQDs), only o-phenylenediamine (oPDA) was used as a precursor. To obtain Y-NCQDs, 2 mmol oPDA was dispersed in 10 mL DI water and reacted in a microwave oven at 180 °C for 10 min. After being allowed to cool down to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 13500 rpm for 10 min and filtered by a 0.22 μm PTFE membrane filter, followed by dialysis with a MWCO 3.5 kDa for 48 h to remove unreacted precursors. Meanwhile, for the synthesis of R-NCQDs, oPDA was dispersed in 10 mL DI water followed by the addition of 200 μL HCl and graphitization was proceeded in a microwave oven at 180 °C for 10 min. Once the reaction was finished, the obtained dark-brown solution was neutralized with NaOH solution. The precipitates were obtained by centrifugation at 13500 rpm for 10 min and dispersed in DI water, then dialyzed with a MWCO 3.5 kDa dialysis tube for 48 h to remove unreacted precursors. Finally, the dark powdered R-NCQDs were obtained by freeze drying for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of full-color light conversion NCQDs films
ce:section.ce:para: 15 g of Sylgard 184 silicone elastomer base (PDMS base) and 1.5 g of Sylgard 184 silicone elastomer curing agent (PDMS curing agent) were mixed with 1.5 mL of NCQDs solution (10 mg ml−1) and stirred vigorously for 3 mins. The NCQDs@PDMS composites dispersions were then poured into a petri dish and placed in a vacuum desiccator to release the gas inside the solution for 60 mins. The NCQDs films were finally obtained by curing at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of NCQD-based light emitting diodes
ce:section.ce:para: Commercial AlGaInP chips with 365 nm emission were used as a light source, and the NCQDs@PDMS composites dispersions were coated with AlGaInP chips to use them as color conversion layers. The dispersions were dropped onto the AlGaInP chip. Then, NCQDs-based light-emitting diodes (NCQDs-LEDs) were finally fabricated through drying them at 60 °C for 12 h.

UV absorption was measured with a Jasco V-670 UV–VIS-NIR spectrophotometer at the Future Energy Convergence Core center (FECC). PL spectra were measured with a Jasco FP-6500 spectrofluorometer. Raman spectra were measured with a Nanophoton Raman spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm at the Center for University-Wide Research Facilities, Jeonbuk National University (CURF, JBNU). The FT-IR spectrum was measured with Jasco FT/IR-4100 Infrared spectroscopy at the Future Energy Convergence Core center (FECC). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope at the Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST). Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were measured with a HORIBA Fluorolog-QM TCSPC spectrofluorometer at the Future Energy Convergence Core center (FECC). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) were measured with a NEXSA hybrid X-ray photoelectron spectrometer system at the Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of multicolor light emissive nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Multicolor light emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were successfully produced through a microwave-assisted synthetic process by selecting citric acid (CA), urea (UA), and o-phenylenediamine (oPDA) without any further purification steps. As shown in Scheme 1 , for the synthesis of blue and green light emissive NCQDs (B- and G-NCQDs), the light emissions of NCQDs were controlled by adjusting only the molar ratios of CA to UA from 1 to 6, respectively. CA was used as a carbon bonding source and UA was used as a nitrogen doping source, which are mainly used to synthesize blue- and green-light emissive CQDs via a hydrothermal or solvothermal method [35,36]. Yellow-light emissive NCQDs (Y-NCQDs) were obtained when only oPDA was used, while red-light emissive NCQDs (R-NCQDs) were synthesized in the presence of diluted HCl as an acid catalyst to accelerate the graphitization of CQDs. For both Y- and R-NCQDs, oPDA was used as both a nitrogen doping source and a carbon source to form sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon bonding because it has aromatic rings and two amine groups as a functional group. Their multicolor light emissions were shown under UV light irradiation at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Scheme 1).

As shown in Scheme S1, the multicolor light emissive NCQDs were prepared by a condensation between carboxylic acid of CA and amine groups of UA. Then, the carbonization were proceeded under a microwave irradiation, resulting in the formation of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon bonding and graphitic nitrogen in the core. Higher molar ratio of CA to UA facilitated the formation of relative higher sp2 -carbon bonding and graphitic nitrogen in the core of CQD, resulting in the red-shifted light emission of NCQDs from blue to green. Since oPDA has an aromatic ring and an amine group as a functional group, it serves as both a carbon precursor and a nitrogen doping source to form a 2,3-diaminophenzine (DAP) structure to synthesize yellow-light emissive NCQDs [37]. When oPDA was used as a precursor, the resulting NCQDs can be produced to emit different colors according to their isomers such as ortho-, meta-, para-PDA. Otherwise, oPDA were only used for producing blue, green, and red light emissive NCQDs by adjusting reaction temperature and acidity of solvents [38–41]. With this regards, in order to synthesize red-light emissive NCQDs, a 200 μL of HCl was added into reaction solutions as a catalyst to form increased sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon bonding and nitrogen doping in the core. These will be demonstrated in deeper detail in other sections. Further, we confirmed our microwave-assisted synthetic process to produce multicolor light emission of NCQDs without a further purification process by choosing the right precursors with the optimal molar ratios in the presence of catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of multicolor light emission of NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of NCQDs, such as their absorbance, photoluminescence (PL), and photoluminescent excitation (PLE), were investigated. The absorption peaks of NCQDs below 350 nm were commonly attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and CN bonds occurring in sp2 -conjugations and the n-π* transitions of CO and CO bonds occurring in defect states (Fig. 1 a–d) [42]. Moreover, the absorption peaks of NCQDs from 400 nm to 600 nm were attributed to the transitions of the amine groups of surface [37]. The PL spectra having emission peaks for B-, G-, Y- and R-NCQDs at 445 nm, 545 nm, 570 nm, and 600 nm, respectively, were overlapped with the absorption spectra, indicating that the emissions from NCQDs occurred in the transition of absorption bands (Fig. 1). Furthermore, NCQDs have peaks in the same emission wavelengths according to the different excitation wavelengths; B-, G-, Y- and R-NCQDs at 445 nm, 545 nm, 570 nm, and 600 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). The absolute QYs for B-, G-, Y-, and R-NCQDs were measured to be 8.6 %, 11 %, 3.1 %, and 14.5 %, respectively. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra determined that the average photon lifetimes of B-, G-, Y-, and R-NCQDs were 4.31 ns, 4.18 ns, 1.12 ns, and 1.21 ns when observed at excitation wavelengths of 375 nm, 450 nm, 450 nm and 534 nm, respectively, which indicates that the time fluorophore resides in the excited state before giving a photon and returning to the ground state (Fig. S2).

The Tauc plot, using the equation Eg opt = 1240/λedge, based on the UV–vis spectrum of NCQDs revealed that the bandgap energies of B-, G-, Y-, and R-NCQDs were calculated to be 3.46 eV, 2.69 eV, 2.64 eV, and 2.08 eV, respectively (Fig. S3). Further, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level was investigated by using ultraviolet photoelectron spectra (UPS), and it was calculated using the equation of EHOMO = hν − (Ecutoff − Eonset) (Fig. 2 ). The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level was calculated based on the bandgap energy from Tauc plots and the HOMO energy level. The HOMO, LUMO, and energy bandgap are summarized in Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 2e, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of NCQDs ranged from −5.29 eV to −1.39 eV, and the bandgap energy tends to narrow with the red-shifted PL emission of NCQDs. These optical properties are affected by the physicochemical features of NCQDs, which will be discussed in the next section.

The morphologies of the multicolor light emissive NCQDs were characterized using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). The multicolor light emissive NCQDs have size distributions ranging from 3 to 5 nm, wherein their average size of B-NCQDs is 4.03 nm (Fig. 3 a). The inset of HRTEM image of NCQDs showed circular shapes of NCQDs with a visible crystalline lattice fringe. The lattice spacing of 0.34 nm corresponded to the (002) facet of graphite. The additional TEM analysis show that B-, G-, Y-, and R-NCQDs have very similar diameters ranges from 3 to 5 nm, which indicates that the quantum confinement effect (QCE) was not a dominant parameter to manipulate the bandgap of NCQDs (Fig. S4).

To shed light on the mechanism of multicolor light-emission of NCQDs, the Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were also performed. The origin of PL emission from the NCQDs with different wavelengths can be investigated by comparing the graphitization degrees. As shown in Fig. S6a, the Raman spectra of NCQDs have two distinctive bands from 1351 cm−1 to 1375 cm−1 (D band, sp3 carbon bonding) and from 1572 cm−1 to 1600 cm−1 (G band, sp2 -carbon bonding). The relative intensity ratios of ID/IG, which means crystallinity, were 0.93, 0.91, 1.19 and 1.47 for B-. G-, Y-, and R-NCQDs, respectively. In comparing B- and G-NCQDs, the higher molar ratio of CA:UA facilitated the formation of a higher intrinsic sp2 -hybridized structure, which causes the bandgap to narrow [42]. When it comes to the Y- and R-NCQDs, in addition to the D band and G band, the Raman spectra showed several peaks at 1150 cm−1 and 1258 cm−1 and in the range of 1300–1600 cm−1, which are attributed to the C-N heterocyclic structure [42]. As a result, the Raman spectra of Y- and R-NCQDs showed that the graphitic nitrogen was doped into the C-N heterocyclic structure in the NCQDs, indicating that the nitrogen doping was more dominant than the crystallinity of the NCQDs in modulating the bandgaps. XPS analysis was conducted to investigate the effects of elemental compositions on the tunable optical bandgaps. The XPS survey spectra showed three distinctive peaks at 284.4, 399.4, and 532.0 eV for C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. S5). As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s for multicolor light emissive NCQDs have three energy binding peaks at 284.4, 285.7, and 288.1 eV, which can be respectively assigned to the CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO bonds. Significantly, there is no CO peak in Y-NCQDs, which is attributed to the fact that oPDA reacts and forms hydroxyl groups, but nor carboxyl or ketone groups. However, there is a CO peak in R-NCQDs, because HCl activates the amine groups of oPDA to form carboxylates (Scheme S1). High-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s for the NCQDs has three energy binding peaks at 399.3, 400.3, and 401.4 eV, which respectively indicate pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen (Fig. 4b). The relative compositions of carbon and nitrogen were summarized; the percentage of CC/CC bonding and graphitic nitrogen was increased with the red-shifted PL of NCQDs (Table S1). The results of XPS analysis showed that both the higher molar ratio of CA to UA and the presence of diluted HCl as a catalyst facilitated the formation of sp2 /sp3 hybridized carbon bonding and graphitic nitrogen in the core of CQDs. Therefore, it is necessary to control the sp2 /sp3 hybridized carbon bonding and have graphitic nitrogen in the core of CQDs to manipulate the bandgaps. Further, the FT-IR spectra were obtained to investigate the surface functional groups on the NCQDs. The FT-IR spectra of NCQDs showed that they have the following common functional groups: the broad absorption band at 3100–3500 cm−1 is attributed to ν(OH) and ν(NH) in the hydroxyl and amine groups. The absorption band at 1710 cm−1 is attributed to ν(CO) in carboxyl groups. The absorption band at 1500–1700 cm−1 is assigned to the aromatic structures (CC, CN) of NCQDs (Fig. S6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor light emissive NCQDs-based light-emitting diodes
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained multicolor light emissive NCQDs were employed in photoluminescent applications such as light conversion films and WLEDs. The NCQDs films were obtained by mixing NCQDs solution with PDMS elastomer and PDMS curing agent in a ratio of 9 to 1. The NCQDs-based LED were fabricated by using an AlGaInP UV LED chip as a light source with an emission wavelength of 365 nm and coating with NCQDs composite films. First, each different color emission of CQDs was homogeneously dispersed in PDMS resin, resulting in transparent NCQDs@PDMS films. The light conversion of those NCQDs@PDMS films was investigated under UV light irradiation at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S7a-d). The NCQDs@PDMS films were examined by X-ray diffraction to investigate the effect of addition of NCQDs in amorphous PDMS, resulted in no distinctive XRD patterns between pure PDMS and PDMS composites with NCQDs due to the low contents of NCQDs; a broad band around 2θ = 11.6° (Fig. S8). Then, the multicolor light emitting diodes were prepared by using each of NCQDs/PDMS polymer matrices as phosphor coating on AlGaInP UV LED chips. As shown in Fig. 5 , B-, G-, Y-, and R-LEDs emit photoluminescent blue, green, yellow, and red light with the CIE coordinates of (0.16, 0.13), (0.38, 0.58), (0.48, 0.51), and (0.62, 0.38), respectively.

To fabricate WLEDs, B-, G-, and R-NCQDs as phosphors were mixed in varying ratios and coated with AlGaInP UV LED chips. In the mass ratio of 1:1:2 of B-, G-, and R-NCQDs, WLEDs emitted cool white light with a CCT of 7151 K (cool WLEDs) and CIE coordinates of (0.30, 0.33) (Fig. 6 a). Cool WLEDs are mainly used for outdoor lighting or in workplaces that require concentration. In a mass ratio of 1:2:4 of B-, G-, and R-NCQDs, WLEDs emitted natural white light with CCT of 5916 K (natural WLEDs) and CIE coordinates of (0.32, 0.36) (Fig. 6b). Natural WLEDs are intended to appear similar to daylight and are mainly used in bathrooms or workplaces. In mass ratio of 1:2:10 of B-, G-, and R-NCQDs, WLEDs emitted warm white light with CCT of 3386 K (warm WLEDs) and CIE coordinates of (0.40, 0.37) (Fig. 6c). Warm WLEDs are mainly used for indoor lighting or in areas of a house that require light that can comfortably be seen by the eyes. As shown in Fig. 6d–f, the relative intensity ratio of blue, green, and red emission determined the value of CCT of WLEDs. In cool WLEDs, the intensity of the blue emission was similar to those of the green and red emissions, which indicated bluish white light. Otherwise, in warm WLEDs, the intensity of the blue and green emission was lower than that of red emission, thus indicating orange white light. Therefore, we fabricated CCT-adjustable WLEDs from 3386 K to 7151 K by employing different light emissions of NCQDs with various mixing ratios.

In conclusion, multicolor light emission of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were produced using a microwave-assisted synthetic process without a further purification process. The different light emissions from NCQDs came from the surface states, such as the functional groups on the surface of NCQDs and the types of bonding structures. This was achieved by adjusting the molar ratios of citric acid and urea from 1 to 6 for blue and green-light emission, respectively. The yellow- and red-light emission from NCQDs were achieved by using o-phenylenediamine (oPDA) as a precursor in the presence of a diluted HCl as a catalyst, which accelerates sp2 /sp3 hybridized carbon bonding and extends graphitic nitrogen doping in the core of NCQDs. It was concluded that the sp2 /sp3 hybridized carbon bonding and graphitic nitrogen in the core led to the red-shifted PL emissions through XPS measurement. Multicolor light emissive NCQDs-based LEDs were fabricated by using NCQDs/PDMS polymer matrices as a conversion layer on the commercial UV LED chips. The NCQDs-based white-light emitting diodes (WLEDs) were fabricated by adjusting the mixing ratios of NCQDs with different color emissions. WLED showed the correlated color temperature (CCT) from 3386 K to 7151 K and CIE coordinates of (0.30, 0.32), (0.32, 0.36), and (0.40, 0.37).
The manuscript was written with contributions from all authors. All the authors approved the final version of the manuscript.

Bo-Seok Kim: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Geun-Hyeong Oh: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Youngjun Song: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Youn-Sik Lee: Funding acquisition, Resources, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Sejung Kim: Funding acquisition, Resources, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Sejung Kim reports financial support was provided by National Research Foundation of Korea. Sejung Kim reports a relationship with Jeonbuk National University that includes: employment and funding grants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (NRF-2021R1F1A1063451 and NRF-2021R1A43031992).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.158301

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrabroad-band, red sufficient, solid white emission from carbon quantum dot aggregation for single component warm white light emitting diodes with a 91 high color rendering index

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Meng, T. Yuan, X. Li, Y. Li, L. Fan, S. Yang, Ultrabroad-band, red sufficient, solid white emission from carbon quantum dot aggregation for single component warm white light emitting diodes with a 91 high color rendering index, Chem. Commun. 55(46) (2019) 6531-6534.
